options.content:
# W&B Data Stores (app view)

Answers **"is the database or cache the bottleneck — from the W&B application's point of view?"** These panels come from the *app's* instrumentation (how long its own DB operations take, how hard it is throttling itself, how well its caches are working), **not** from the stores' own process metrics.

**For the stores' own CPU / memory / disk / server-side locks** (MySQL, Redis, ClickHouse, Kafka, SeaweedFS) use the [W&B Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance) dashboard.

**Read the sections top to bottom:** (1) **Operation latency & errors** — is the app's view of the store slow or erroring? (2) **MySQL limiter pressure** — is the app throttling its own DB access? (3) **Caches** — are caches absorbing load, or is everything falling through to MySQL?

Use the **W&B Namespace** dropdown above to switch installs.

panels.title: Operation latency p95 by operation (top 10)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Histogram operation_duration (_bucket/_sum/_count) with tags operation, optype, item, impl, code.

options.content:
**What it shows** — p95 latency of the app's data-store operations, one line per `operation` (top 10). This is how slow the store *feels* to W&B, including client-side queuing.

**What bad looks like** — one operation's p95 climbing well above its normal band, or many operations rising together (a shared store-wide slowdown).

**Do next** — many operations slow at once → check **MySQL limiter pressure** below and the store's own resources on [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance). A single slow operation → **Open Traces in Explore** (top-right) and filter to that operation to find the slow query.

panels.title: Operation errors per sec by operation & code (top 10)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Counter operation_errors with tags operation, optype, item, impl, code.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the rate of failed data-store operations, split by `operation` and error `code`.

**What bad looks like** — any sustained non-zero line, especially a code that maps to timeouts, deadlocks, or connection exhaustion.

**Do next** — errors that look like contention (deadlocks, lock waits) → check **MySQL limiter pressure** below and the store's server-side locks on [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance). Otherwise snapshot this panel with the failing operation/code and escalate.

panels.title: Limiter concurrency & per-request query time
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Gauge mysql_limiter_max_attempted_concurrency and histogram mysql_limiter_per_request_query_seconds (_bucket/_sum/_count).

options.content:
**What it shows** — how hard the app is pushing MySQL: peak attempted concurrency and how long each throttled request's query takes (avg + p95).

**What bad looks like** — attempted concurrency pinned at its ceiling for long stretches, and/or per-request query p95 climbing — the app wants more DB throughput than the limiter allows.

**Do next** — sustained ceiling + rising query time → MySQL is the bottleneck. Confirm on the store side (CPU, threads_running, lock waits) via [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance), then snapshot both and escalate.

panels.title: Limiter acquires, unbalanced & deadlocks (per sec)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Counters mysql_limiter_total_attempted_acquires, mysql_limiter_unbalanced, deadlocks, dual_write.

options.content:
**What it shows** — rate of limiter acquire attempts, `unbalanced` events (uneven load across limiter shards), `deadlocks`, and `dual_write` (write path fan-out).

**What bad looks like** — `deadlocks` above zero (contention on the same rows), or `unbalanced` climbing (some shards hot while others idle).

**Do next** — sustained deadlocks → correlate with the **Operation errors** panel and check server-side lock waits (`mysql_global_status_innodb_row_lock_current_waits`) on [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance). Snapshot + escalate if persistent.

panels.title: Cache hit ratio
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Counters cache_hit and cache_miss.

options.content:
**What it shows** — the overall cache hit ratio: hits / (hits + misses). Closer to 1.0 is better.

**What bad looks like** — hit ratio **dropping** off its normal baseline. That means more requests fall through to MySQL, which drives up the latency and limiter pressure seen above.

**Do next** — a falling hit ratio → check the **MySQL limiter pressure** section above and the store's load on [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance). A sudden drop after a deploy → check **Service Image Versions** on [Field Investigation](/d/wandb-field-investigation).

panels.title: Cache activity by type (per sec)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Counters generic_cache_hit, db2_prepare_cache_hit/_miss, footer_hit/_miss, lru_evict.

options.content:
**What it shows** — per-cache hit/miss rates for the specific caches the app uses (generic, db2 prepared-statement, footer) plus LRU evictions.

**What bad looks like** — a specific cache's `miss` overtaking its `hit`, or `lru evict` spiking (the cache is too small / churning), which pushes load onto MySQL.

**Do next** — identify which cache is missing most, then correlate with the **hit ratio** and **limiter** panels. If evictions are high and MySQL is under pressure, snapshot + escalate for a cache-sizing review.

panels.title: Cache hit duration (avg / p95)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Histogram cache_hit_duration (_bucket/_sum/_count).

options.content:
**What it shows** — how long it takes to serve a value *from* cache (avg and p95). Even hits cost something.

**What bad looks like** — hit duration climbing well above its normal band — the cache backend itself (e.g. Redis) is slow, so even cache hits hurt.

**Do next** — if hit duration is rising, check Redis on [Managed Install Performance](/d/wandb-managed-install-performance) (`redis_*` metrics). Snapshot + escalate if the cache backend is degraded.

panels.title: Cache entries (gauge)
panels.description: TODO: DogStatsD metric — currently EMPTY because gorilla runs with GORILLA_STATSD_PORT=0, which disables DogStatsD emission (a core/manifest-side setting, not the operator); it populates once that port is set. Names may carry a service prefix; if still empty after enabling, check label_values(__name__). Gauge cache_entries (per-cache label may be impl).

options.content:
**What it shows** — the number of entries currently held in each cache.

**What bad looks like** — entry count pinned flat at a round ceiling while `lru evict` (panel above) is high — the cache is full and constantly evicting. A sudden drop to zero can mean the cache was flushed/restarted.

**Do next** — full-and-evicting → correlate with the **hit ratio** drop and **limiter** pressure; this is the signal for a cache-sizing review. Snapshot the three cache panels together + escalate.
